text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a large uterus, beginning by cutting the right round ligament with a harmonic ace to increase lateral mobility. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened using the Clermont-Ferrand technique to increase the distance between the uterus and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament. The IP ligament is controlled below the ovary with bipolar current, allowing safe cutting away from the right ureter. The same procedure is repeated on the left side, including cutting the left round ligament, opening the anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament, and coagulating and cutting the left IP ligament.

The surgeon begins the vesicovaginal dissection by cutting the anterior leaf of the brown ligament towards the bladder, using cranial traction to cut the isthmic peritoneum and create space between the bladder and cervical isthmic area. The anterior peritoneum is cut, and surrounding tissues on the right uterine pedicle are removed. Dissection continues towards the right uterosacral ligament to increase uterine mobility. The median part of the bladder pillar is cut to free the bladder and expose the anterior part of the uterine manipulator. The left uterine artery is controlled but not cut to manage backflow.

The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle with bipolar cautery and cuts the artery using the harmonic ace, achieving a bloodless procedure. The dissection continues toward the right uterosacral ligament, repeating the same steps on the left side. The left uterine pedicle, previously coagulated, is cut along with the uterine vein and the cranial part of the left uterosacral ligament. A monopolar needle is used to perform a colpotomy, cutting the anterior, lateral, and posterior aspects of the vagina in a bloodless manner.

The surgeon performs intra-abdominal morcellation of the uterus using a Chardonnay knife, a 10mm cold blade device. The uterus is divided into two parts, which are then extracted using a spooler ball and laparoscopic palsy forceps. The large volume of the uterus is visible after vaginal extraction.

The surgeon begins the vaginal closure using a 14mm needle with Vial Zero suture, placing the first stitch on the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to reduce the risk of vaginal prolapse. The closure involves interrupted sutures on the left and right aspects of the vagina, ensuring the entire vaginal mucosa is included to minimize post-operative bleeding. The surgeon uses intracorporeal knotting technique to achieve a secure closure.
